contents.value:
```awk
# input: "John 30 Engineer"
#   $0 = "John 30 Engineer"  (whole line)
#   $1 = "John"              (1st field)
#   $NF = "Engineer"         (last field)
#   NF = 3                   (field count)
```
**Fields** — Awk splits lines by FS (default: whitespace). Access with `$1`, `$2`, etc.

```awk
/error/  { print }          # regex match
$3 > 100 { print $1, $3 }   # field condition
NR == 1  { print "Header:", $0 }
END      { print sum }       # final output
```
**pattern { action }** is the fundamental awk structure.

```bash
awk '{print $2}' file.txt            # print 2nd column
awk '{s+=$1} END{print s}' data.txt  # sum column
awk -F, 'NR>1 {print $1}' data.csv   # CSV skip header
awk '!seen[$0]++' file.txt           # unique lines
```
**One-liners** — awk excels as a command-line data processor.

```awk
printf "%-20s %5d %8.2f\n", $1, $2, $3
# %s string  %d integer  %f float
# %x hex     %o octal    %e scientific
```
**printf** provides C-style formatted output. No automatic newline.

```awk
NR       # total record (line) number
FNR      # record number in current file
NF       # number of fields in current line
FS       # input field separator (default: space)
OFS      # output field separator
RS       # input record separator (default: newline)
ORS      # output record separator
FILENAME # current input filename
```
**Built-in variables** control how awk reads and writes data.

```awk
BEGIN { FS = "," }     # CSV
BEGIN { FS = "\t" }    # TSV
BEGIN { FS = ":" }     # /etc/passwd
# Or from command line:
# awk -F, '{print $1}' file.csv
```
**FS** (field separator) controls how lines are split into fields. Default is whitespace.

```awk
BEGIN { OFS = "," }
{ print $1, $2, $3 }   # output: field1,field2,field3

# Force recalculation:
{ $1 = $1; print }     # rebuild $0 with OFS
```
**OFS** (output field separator) is used between fields in print. Set `$1=$1` to rebuild `$0`.

```awk
BEGIN { RS = "" }    # paragraph mode (blank line separates records)
BEGIN { RS = ";" }   # semicolon-separated records
```
**RS** (record separator) controls how input is split into records. Default is newline.

```awk
{ print NR, $0 }         # line numbers
NR == 5 { print }        # print 5th line
NR >= 10 && NR <= 20     # lines 10-20
END { print NR, "lines" }
```
**NR** is the total number of records (lines) read so far across all files.

```awk
{ print NF }              # count fields per line
NF > 0 { print }          # skip blank lines
{ print $NF }             # print last field
{ print $(NF-1) }         # print second-to-last
```
**NF** is the number of fields in the current record. `$NF` accesses the last field.

```awk
{ print FILENAME, NR, $0 }
```
**FILENAME** is the name of the current input file.

```awk
# Read next line:
getline line

# Read from file:
getline line < "file.txt"

# Read from command:
"date" | getline today
close("date")
```
**getline** reads the next line. Can read from current input, a file, or a command pipe.

```awk
{ gsub(/old/, "new") }       # replace all in $0
{ gsub(/,/, "\t", $0) }      # CSV to TSV
n = gsub(/pattern/, "rep")   # returns replacement count
```
**gsub** globally substitutes pattern matches. Modifies the target in place. Returns count.

```awk
{ sub(/^[ \t]+/, "") }     # trim leading whitespace
{ sub(/pattern/, "rep") }  # replace first match only
```
**sub** substitutes the first occurrence only. Use `gsub` for all occurrences.

```awk
substr($0, 1, 10)     # first 10 characters
substr($0, 5)         # from position 5 to end
```
**substr(string, start, length)** extracts a substring. Position is 1-based.

```awk
n = split($0, arr, ":")
for (i = 1; i <= n; i++)
    print arr[i]
```
**split(string, array, separator)** splits a string into an array. Returns the number of elements.

```awk
length($0)             # string length
length(arr)            # array size (gawk)
```
**length** returns the number of characters in a string or elements in an array (gawk).

```awk
result = sprintf("%.2f", $1 * 1.08)
formatted = sprintf("%04d", NR)
```
**sprintf** returns a formatted string (like printf but doesn't print).

```awk
{ system("mkdir -p " $1) }
system("echo done")
```
**system(command)** executes a shell command. Returns the exit status.

```awk
{ print $0 > "output.txt" }     # overwrite
{ print $0 >> "append.txt" }    # append
{ print $1 > ($2 ".txt") }      # dynamic filename
```
**Output redirection** — `>` overwrites, `>>` appends. Close files when done.

```awk
{ result = $1 " " $2 }       # space concatenation
{ name = first "_" last }    # with separator
{ s = s $0 "\n" }            # accumulate lines
```
**String concatenation** is done by juxtaposition (placing strings next to each other).

```awk
$0 ~ /pattern/  { print }     # line matches
$1 !~ /^[0-9]/  { print }     # field doesn't match
/^#/            { next }      # skip comments
```
**Regex operators** — `~` matches, `!~` doesn't match. Bare `/regex/` matches against `$0`.

```awk
/^#/ { next }          # skip comment lines
NR == 1 { next }       # skip header
{ process($0) }
```
**next** skips to the next input line, bypassing remaining rules for the current line.

```awk
NR == 100 { exit }          # stop after 100 lines
/STOP/ { exit 1 }           # exit with error code
END { print "done" }        # END still runs after exit
```
**exit** stops processing input. Optional status code. END block still executes.

```bash
# Process multiple files:
awk '{print FILENAME, FNR, $0}' file1.txt file2.txt

# Join two files (like paste):
awk 'FNR==NR {a[FNR]=$0; next} {print a[FNR], $0}' file1 file2
```
**Multiple files** — FNR resets per file, NR keeps counting. `FNR==NR` trick processes first file only.

```awk
BEGIN { OFMT = "%.2f" }
{ print $1 + 0 }   # outputs with 2 decimal places
```
**OFMT** controls the output format for numbers converted to strings. Default is `%.6g`.

```awk
{ count[$1, $2]++ }   # multi-dimensional array key
for (key in count) {
    split(key, parts, SUBSEP)
    print parts[1], parts[2], count[key]
}
```
**SUBSEP** (subscript separator, `\034`) joins multi-dimensional array indices. Split with `SUBSEP` to recover keys.

```awk
delete arr["key"]       # delete one element
delete arr              # delete entire array (gawk)

for (k in arr) delete arr[k]  # portable delete all
```
**delete** removes array elements. `delete arr` clears entire array (gawk extension).

```awk
if ((getline line < "file.txt") < 0)
    print "Error:", ERRNO
```
**ERRNO** (gawk) contains the error message when I/O operations fail.

```awk
# gawk-specific features:
@include "library.awk"       # include files
BEGINFILE { print FILENAME }  # per-file begin
ENDFILE { print FNR, "lines" } # per-file end
switch ($1) { case /pat/: ...; default: ... }
```
**gawk extensions** add includes, BEGINFILE/ENDFILE, switch/case, and more.
